The *cytoplasm* is the term used to refer to everything inside the cell except the nucleus. The cytoplasm, in turn, can be further divided as the *cytosol* (i.e. the water component of the cell or intracellular fluid, which constitutes about 55% of total cell volume) and the *organelles*. The cytosol also contains many components such as ions and the energy sources of glycogen, lipids, proteins, ATP and all of the necessary enzymes needed to maintain important cellular processes. The organelles are structures made up of biomolecules which carry out specific cellular functions.

Let's look more closely at some of the most important cellular organelles.

## Definition

The *cytoplasm* refers to everything inside the cell except the nucleus. It is subdivided into the *cytosol* (the water component or intracellular fluid, constituting about 55% of total cell volume) and the *organelles* (structures made up of biomolecules that carry out specific cellular functions).

## Core Principles

- The cytoplasm is partitioned into two functional compartments: the cytosol (aqueous phase) and the organelles (membrane-bound or biomolecular structures).
- The cytosol occupies roughly 55% of total cell volume, serving as the medium in which soluble cellular components are dissolved or suspended.
- The cytosol holds ions, energy substrates (glycogen, lipids, proteins, ATP), and the enzymes required to sustain essential cellular processes.
- Organelles, composed of biomolecules, execute specialised cellular functions distinct from those carried out in the cytosol.

## Sub-concepts

- **Cytosol** — the intracellular fluid component of the cytoplasm; ~55% of cell volume; contains ions, glycogen, lipids, proteins, ATP, and enzymes.
- **Organelles** — biomolecular structures within the cytoplasm dedicated to specific cellular functions.

## Practical Applications

- The cytosol functions as the reservoir of immediate energy substrates (glycogen, lipids, proteins, ATP) and the enzymatic machinery underpinning metabolic pathways relevant to sport and exercise biochemistry.
